sentences.text: My name is Alex Gross.
The spelling is A-L-E-X G-R-O-S-S.
At birth, I  was given Jankle.
A Jewish name short-- affectionately for Jakob, J-A-K-O-B.
September 18, 1928.
21, But trapped in a 67-year-old body.
I was born in Czechoslovakia in the Carpathian Mountains.
And when I was a young boy, it was Czechoslovakia.
But later on, it became Hungary.
And then it was taken over by Germany.
And then Russia annexed it.
And it's now part of Ukraine.
It's a village right next to Munkacs.
The village was called Palanka or Palanok.
It's-- in Czech, it was spelled P-A-L-O-N-K.
In Hungarian, it was Val-Palanka because it had a big bastion there.
So it was called Val-Palanka.
My mother's name was [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was affectionately called Ettele.
But-- in fact, my oldest daughter is named after her, Etta, E-T-T-A.
My mother's maiden name was Lebowitz.
L-E-B-O-W-I-T-Z.
My father's name was Chaim Akiva Gross.
And he was one of-- first of many brothers.
And so was my mother.
May she rest in peace.
And they come from Lavik, a village on the other side of Munkacs.
Yes, I was fortunate to know my grandmother on my father's side in Lavik.
And in fact, we have a picture of her when she was at a-- once with us.
And I was fortunate-- I believe I was very, very young to know my grandmother.
And she passed away when I was, I think, maybe four or five years old.
And my grandfather, may he rest in peace, he was known throughout the village as a jovial type of person.
He had a big, white beard.
And I-- yes, I got to know him quite well.
He was a very caring-- very, very caring grandfather.
Father was a tailor.
And all of us were basically trained to be tailors.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And grandfather was a-- I don't know what you'd call it here.
But he had a horse and buggy.
And that was his profession.
He transported mainly goods.
And, of course, we had lots of family-- lots of people to feed, lots of children.
Well, we were blessed with six brothers and one sister on mother's side.
Our oldest brother is Philip, who was trapped in Russia for 25 years.
Fortunately, he's out.
And he is now in Canada and America.
And he lost his wife, who was a prisoner in Auschwitz, to cancer.
And the next brother is brother Ben, who lives here in Atlanta.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then there was brother Bernie.
He retired now in, in Florida.
And then there's Bill, who lives in Youngstown, Ohio, and-- followed by Sam, who lives in Tampa, Florida.
And then there's me.
I was the sixth boy.
And then our sister, Rosalyn, who was the youngest and oldest.
And thanks God, she's alive and well.
And she's here in Atlanta.
Our religious background was very Orthodox, very traditional.
There's-- there was no such thing as not going on Shabbos to shul-- to the synagogue.
But we were not considered the ultra-Orthodox.
Father didn't make us wear payos.
And we were allowed to have hair.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were more or less regular boys.
But most of the other families in our village in the town were very, very Orthodox, ultra-Orthodox.
Well, [SIGHS] Friday afternoon, we had to, more or less, knock off and get ready, go to the mikveh, and come back, get dressed, and walk to shul.
And after shul, we used to come home and have our Sabbath meal.
And, of course, after the meal, we had to sing [NON-ENGLISH].
And then go to bed.
And next morning without any question, all of us went to shul.
Some of the older boys didn't go as early as we did.
Father used to take us very early because he was the gabbai of the shul.
And basically, we had to be there as young boys and pray alongside of him.
As a matter of fact, my cousin and I were singing and, more or less, like a choir for the-- for the cantor for the holidays.
And we had to go to cheder.
But on Sabbath when we came back home, we had our big, big meal of the week.
It was like, really a feast.
It was the only real meal that we had during the week.
Well, we were very fortunate that-- because he was a tailor.
He was able to bargain and get a chicken and, of course-- or sometimes even roast beef or even steaks.
Because I was the youngest of the boys.
And we had at least four or five people always working for us.
By the time the plate came to me, there wasn't that much left.
But we were very, very nice.
Everybody just took so much of their food and, and made sure none of us ever went hungry.
Mother was an exceptional cook.

In fact, most of the boys from the village used to look forward to Sabbath because they used to come to our home [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the pretense that they want to come and sing [NON-ENGLISH] with us.
But not only that, they also enjoyed mother's cakes or cookies or pies, or whatever she had.
It, it was a very poor subsistence, but very gracious at the same time.
We had a lot of togetherness.
Yes, and in fact, I didn't know they were not part of the family.
And fortunately, brother Bernie married a girl.
And one of the guys that worked for us, he married her sister.
And we're still together.
Unfortunately, his wife died of cancer.
She was also part of Dr.
Mengele's experiments in Auschwitz.
And his brother always used to-- also worked for us.
And there's a guy by the name of Barry.
And in fact, I, I will show you a picture of-- his brother married my aunt, my mother's sister.
She, her husband, and the three children all perished in Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never knew he wasn't a brother-- Barry.
And his nephew also worked for us, us, [NON-ENGLISH], who used to live in Worcester, Ohio.
And then we had another guy from [NON-ENGLISH] that worked for us.
I really didn't know that they were not brothers.
We were so--
[LAUGHS] To describe it today is very difficult, because our home-- I went back there last year.
I took  my two daughters.
And, of course, they destroyed our home.
But it was so small, the area that that home was in that one of the rooms is much bigger than the whole home was.
And that included the kitchen and everything else.
And I don't know how we kept so strict kosher.
But we did.
Of course, the table where my father used to cut the cloth for tailoring opened up.
And that was-- became a bed at night.
And we had, where they were sleeping-- they were sitting, the customers, that opened up.
And that became a bed.
And two or three-- us, us used to sleep there.
And I went back to where the boys slept.
And there's always-- at least 8, 10 boys slept in two tiny little rooms.
I don't know how in the world they managed to-- you know, not to be on top of each other.
But in today's modern world, it was very difficult for me to understand how in the world we managed to fit in that many.
Oh, I don't even remember.
I must have been awful little.
I must have been probably two or three years old.
The, the, the cheder, unfortunately, our Lehrers, the teachers, were very, very rough, very mean.
Oh, I used to get some mean beatings.
In fact, my brothers one time took a revenge on the teacher because I came home.
And I was bleeding from the-- from the skin off my hands that the teacher beat me up.
No, he was just a, a mean, mean guy.
And, and because I couldn't learn fast enough, I guess, maybe he took his frustration out on me.
But he was a mean, mean guy.
And finally, he got his revenge.
My brothers got their revenge on him.
Well, they locked him up in a barn with a-- with a woman that-- [LAUGHS] as a result of that, they banished him from the village.
But the, the, the schools in those days were not like today.
The teachers were not as qualified as they are today.
And they weren't so concerned about teaching really as, as-- was what they wanted.
And I, I don't have good memories of that.
We had only one sport in those days.
And that was soccer.
And we used to get into fights.
Because as I was growing up, fascism started setting into our area.
And our village was mostly Schwabisch of German descent.
And they took a lot of joy out of attacking us Jewish kids.
And in fact, one time, they broke my leg, they were so mean.
Well, kicking me.
And I couldn't tell father because it was on Shabbos that I played soccer.
[LAUGHS] I would have gotten a worse beating even.
But--
I was probably seven years old, eight years old.
Oh, yes.
Anti-Semitism was something that was going on there constantly, but never openly until Hungary annexed our area.
I was about 11 years old.
And suddenly, all the boys age 11 had to report for military training except the Jewish kids.
Instead of paramilitary training, they taught us how to dig ditches, how to sweep streets.
And most of all, they taught the Christian kids to march past us, to spit at us, kick us, egg us on, throw stones at us.
So it was very humiliating.
And the Gross brothers had a reputation.
Leave us alone.
We'll leave you alone.
And if anybody ever attacked us, we made sure that they will never do it again.
So many times we were attacked.
For instance-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for instance, I was coming back with my brother Sam from cheder.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were attacked by about 10, 15 of the Hitler Youth, which the Hitler-Jugend-- or the Hitler Youth-- area was right next to our home.
And they pounced on us.
And we were in a ditch.
And Sam and I managed to get some stones and somehow crawled under the mud and started bashing their heads in.
And we left all of them bleeding there.
Of course, I had to run away from home-- first to my aunt and uncle in, in Vari where I brought up to you that my aunt, and uncle, and their children all perished in Auschwitz.
And I came back.
And--
Well, we had-- it was quite a, a long way.
And, of course, I had no car, no bicycle.
So I had to hitch a ride here and there.
I had to go to Munkacs, and from there to Beregsaz.
And from Beregsaz, I walked all the way to Vari.
And--
Oh, they, they--
--would have killed me for sure.
And when I came back, they found out after a while that I came-- you know, that I was back.
So they attacked me again.
Well, my brother Sam and I were going to take care of our cow that was grazing.
And we had some straw there and hay.
And we were going to rake it.
And, and he had a pitchfork.
And I had a rake.
And they attacked us.
And fortunately, we made our stand.
We run to a small bridge.
And we made our stand.
We left all of them bleeding.
And we ran away again.
And I did this one more time.
And then I finally had to-- disappeared from the area.
And I left and went to the capital of Hungary, Budapest.
I went to Lavik to-- where we had an aunt and uncle.
Our uncle in Lavik had, I believe, seven or eight kids.
And one of them is still alive-- the only one that survived.
And our aunt had 13 children.
And only one survived.
And he was trapped in Russia for about 28 years.
And when he finally came out to Israel, he got sick.
And he died there.
But he was the only survivor of the entire family.
Not one other one survived.
I stayed with them probably about a month or so.
But when I finally ran away to Budapest, which was the capital of Hungary, I ran there because my brother Ben was there.
And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, I-- in those days, an apprentice didn't make any money.
There was no minimum wages.
So the only way I, I was able to stay sustain myself is getting job-- jobs, you know, pushing crippled people around, elderly people.
And I was fortunate enough to get a position in the famous Boys Choir of Budapest-- the [NON-ENGLISH] Boys Choir.
And the reason I was so fortunate-- not because I had such a good voice, but because I was able to sing in Yiddish.
And they gave me a lead to sing [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] in there.
And-- but the reason it was so good because they used to give us free cookies and milk.
So that helped sustain me.
And when I was back in Budapest last year-- because I retraced my entire routes-- I realized how difficult it was to have, have to carry that heavy, heavy plumber's box.
It was real, real heavy.
And I was still a young boy.
Yeah.
But those toolboxes were heavy.
And one time, it was icy.
And I had to come back from Buda to Pest.
And it's all hill-- down.
And so I put the box down on the floor and dragged it.
And by the time I got to the shop, when the boss inspected the box, he saw there were some scratches underneath.
He almost killed me.
[LAUGHS] So I lost that job.
So I had to find another job.
[LAUGHS]
I found another plumber's apprentice job.
And I stayed there till I was about 15.
Then I came back home.
And by that time, all hell broke loose.
The Jews not only were attacked, and, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] done everything that you could do wrong-- or, or, or the damage to the Jews.
We were being-- we were being robbed and attacked constantly.
And this was about Passover time.
I think it was the last day of Passover, 5 o'clock in the morning.
There was a knock on the door-- excuse me, a week or so before Passover.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was another knock on the door at 5 o'clock.
And somebody tried to come and arrest my father and take him away.
And somehow Rosalyn saw it, my sister, Rosalyn, who was the youngest.
And she cried, and she screamed.
And finally, the rest of us arrived, the rest of the brothers.
And we managed to get rid of that guy.
They tried to take over my father's business.
And-- but this was about the last day of Passover when they knocked on our door.
And they gave us five minutes to pack.
And, you know, they kept on telling us, look, we're going to take you away from these local, Jew-hating anti-Semites.
We're going to relocate you to Germany where we're going to give you jobs.
And you're going to be away from constant harassment.
And, and you'll be able to get food.
At worst, you didn't get ration coupons here.
You couldn't even buy food.
So this went on till-- to a point where we were almost resigned to the fact that, look, they don't take us away.
They ordered us to assemble to the center of the village and then marched us to the ghetto.
But what do you pack in five minutes?
Food?
Valuables?
Clothing?
We didn't know what to do.
We were still very, very cold.
And-- Do you remember anything that you took personally?
No, I don't.
Because the only thing I remember taking is, I think, a blanket [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that mother asked me to take.
I don't remember anything else.
And, yes, I had my best suit on.
For the most part, I had hand-me-downs.
And suddenly, we wound up in the ghetto, which was nothing but a former brick factory.
And I took pictures of the ghetto.
And I have those pictures.
The ghetto was in Munkacs-- between Munkacs and Palanka, our village and, and the main city.
And they had no, no shelters there, no homes, nothing.
We were fortunate because we were one of the first ones there.
We cleaned out the oven where they used to bake and make the bricks.
So we stayed in there.
But it was still sod.
And, you know, it was-- it was kind of very bad.
But we-- about 10 families squeezed into that oven.
Did you know the other families that were with you?
Yeah, most, most of the people were from our village.
And some were from other villages.
[SIGHS] Well, they kept on bringing every day new people in.
I don't know.
I have no idea how many people.
Must have been hundreds and hundreds of people.
Basically, by that time, the German Army took over our area.
So there were both SS men as well as the Nazi Hungarian soldiers guarding us.
And the thing that I remember mostly about the ghetto is the humiliation that they put us through, forcing us to throw bricks at each other-- catch 'em our bare hands.
And, and I went back because I remember how rough those bricks were-- how it took my skin off.
And after about an hour or two, I had no skin left on my hand.
And God forbid, if you dropped a brick, the SS men were there to trample over you or maybe even shoot you.
So it was a very painful memory that I invoked when I went back, how horrible it was.
Because after they forced us to throw all the brick and pack it up in the end of the ghetto, when it was through, they forced us to, to throw them back again and stack them up on the other end.
Did they make the women and girls do this as well?
No, but they made them do some humiliating things-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] washing mud clothing, just nothing but to humiliate them.
Nothing.
They gave us nothing to eat except what we brought with us.
And, you know, after a couple days, the food started running out, you know, because we weren't prepared for it.
They finally gave us a little bit of bread.
And that's it.
One evening, one of the neighbors, who was a very dear friend, he came and threw us over some food because he knew we were-- we had no food at all with a little note.
And he says, I pray for you.
That's a non-Jewish neighbor, yes.
Yeah, it was [NON-ENGLISH].
But I also remember my dearest, dearest friend from the village, Jonczi, across the street.
He and I were inseparable.
We were playing together soccer all the time.
And we were so close before that it wasn't even funny.
But because his background was Schwabisch, he immediately joined the Hitler Youth.
And he became an SS man.
And he became an Obersturmfuhrer.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the horrible part I remember is that he became the worst Jew-hater and the worst Nazi that ever lived.
Unfortunately, I found out that after the war, the Russians captured him.
And they hanged him.
Oh, he was absolutely, unbelievably horrible.
He, he, he made monkeys out of us.
He made all kinds of nasty remarks, and-- not directly at me, at other Jews.
Well, yeah, one time after an incident, he and I slept on top of a haystack.
It was in the summer.
And I asked him, Jonczi, how could you do that?
You're my best friend.
He says, I love you.
But you're a Jew.
And Hitler says that the [SPEAKING GERMAN].
The Jews are our misfortune.
And we've got to dispose of you people so that Germany will become the 1,000-year Reich, because we will control the world for a thousand years.
Because that's what he was taught in the Hitler Youth, that the Jews are the only ones that are in their way to take over the world.
Oh, a lot of them.
Most, most of them-- they did some horrible things.
They, they not only ignored us, but they made sure that we can't buy food.
Food was rationed.
The Jews didn't get coupons.
And some of the neighbors from other villages used to come past our shop and trade with dad for, for work.
And they turned those people in.
So it, it was-- it was unbelievable.
That was probably the most painful thing about that period to see your fellow humans, your friends, suddenly turn and become hateful people where they used to be really ordinary, nice people.
